enrichment.short_description: Counter (counter.dev) is a minimalist, privacy-first web analytics tool that tracks unique visitors and referrers without cookies, logging, or IP fingerprinting.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Counter (counter.dev) is a free, privacy-friendly web analytics tool with no cookies or IP tracking. See features, pricing, and self-hosting options.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Counter, published at counter.dev, is a lightweight, privacy-first web analytics tool created by solo developer Irae Hueck Costa (GitHub handle ihucos) and released around 2020. It was built as a minimalist alternative to Google Analytics for site owners who want basic visitor counts without collecting personal data. Counter explicitly avoids cookies, visitor-level logging, and IP address fingerprinting; instead it relies on aggregate edge signals, such as Cloudflare's country-detection header, to approximate visitor geography without ever storing an IP address. The hosted service at counter.dev is free to use on a pay-what-you-want basis: there is a permanent free tier with no visitor quotas or feature gates, and users who want to support development can opt into voluntary donation tiers starting around 3 euros per month via PayPal. The tracking script is extremely small, roughly 1.1 KB, keeping page-load impact minimal compared with heavier analytics suites. Counter's dashboard focuses on a narrow set of core metrics: unique daily visitors, referrer sources showing which sites link in, and country-level location data, presented in a simple, uncluttered interface rather than the deep funnel and cohort analysis found in enterprise analytics platforms. The full source code is published on GitHub under the ihucos/counter.dev repository, licensed under the GNU Affero General Public License v3 (AGPL-3.0), and a companion self-hosting repository, ihucos/counter.dev-selfhost, provides a Docker-based setup so organizations can run their own instance instead of using the hosted service. The self-hosted stack runs on Go, Redis as the primary hot-tier database, and SQLite for cold archival storage, designed to run efficiently on inexpensive infrastructure; the maintainer has described serving millions of monthly unique visits from a single virtual server costing about five dollars a month. Because Counter does not use cookies or track individuals across sites, it is frequently positioned as a privacy-regulation-friendly option for bloggers, indie developers, small business sites, and privacy-conscious organizations that want basic traffic insight without needing a cookie consent banner. It does not offer the advanced segmentation, event tracking, A/B testing, or product-analytics features found in tools like Countly, Mixpanel, or Google Analytics 4; it is intentionally scoped to simple, aggregate website traffic counting rather than full product analytics. There is no dedicated mobile SDK, no e-commerce tracking, and no built-in advertising attribution, so teams needing those capabilities typically pair Counter with another tool or choose a broader analytics platform instead. Support is community-driven through GitHub issues and public discussion threads rather than a formal enterprise support contract, reflecting its indie, single-maintainer origins. In practice, Counter's value proposition is being a just-the-numbers analytics tool: fast to install with a single script tag, transparent about what it does and does not collect, free for unlimited traffic on the hosted plan, and fully open source for teams that want complete data ownership through self-hosting.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Counter (counter.dev) is a simple, privacy-oriented web analytics tool built by a single independent developer and released publicly around 2020. It was created for people who want to know how many visitors their website gets, and where those visitors come from, without deploying a heavyweight analytics suite or a cookie consent banner.

The tool deliberately limits itself to a small set of aggregate metrics rather than trying to compete with full product-analytics platforms. It does not use cookies, does not log individual visitor behavior over time, and does not store raw IP addresses, relying instead on edge-network signals to approximate a visitor's country.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core features include unique daily visitor counts, referrer tracking to show which sites are sending traffic, and country-level geography, all shown on a minimal real-time dashboard. The JavaScript tracking snippet is about 1.1 KB, so it adds negligible weight to page load times.

The project is fully open source under the AGPL-3.0 license, with the main codebase and a separate self-hosting repository published on GitHub. Self-hosted deployments run on a Go backend with Redis for hot-tier storage and SQLite for archival data, and can be deployed via Docker on modest, low-cost server infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The hosted version at counter.dev is free with no visitor caps or gated features, operating on a pay-what-you-want model; supporters can choose optional donation tiers starting at roughly 3 euros per month. Self-hosting the open-source version is also free aside from the cost of running the small server it requires.

features.description: Tracks daily unique visitors without cookies or persistent visitor identifiers.

features.title: Referrer tracking
features.description: Shows which external sites are sending traffic to a page.

features.title: Country-level geography
features.description: Approximates visitor location using edge network headers instead of storing IP addresses.

features.title: Lightweight tracking script
features.description: A roughly 1.1 KB script tag keeps page-load impact minimal.

features.title: No-cookie tracking
features.description: Avoids cookies, IP fingerprinting, and long-term visitor logging by design.

features.title: Self-hosting option
features.description: Docker-based self-hosted deployment built on Go, Redis, and SQLite.

features.title: Open-source codebase
features.description: Full source published on GitHub under the AGPL-3.0 license.

pros: Free for unlimited traffic on the hosted plan
No cookie consent banner typically required due to its no-cookie approach
Extremely small tracking script with low performance overhead
Fully self-hostable for teams wanting complete data control
Simple, uncluttered dashboard focused on essential metrics
cons: Lacks advanced segmentation, funnels, and event tracking found in full product-analytics tools
No dedicated mobile app SDK
Support is community-driven rather than a formal enterprise contract
Metrics are intentionally limited compared to broader analytics suites

faqs.question: Is Counter free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, the hosted service at counter.dev is free with no visitor quotas, operating on a pay-what-you-want basis with optional donation tiers.

faqs.question: Does Counter use cookies?
faqs.answer: No, Counter is designed to avoid cookies, visitor-level logging, and IP address fingerprinting.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Counter?
faqs.answer: Yes, the project is open source under AGPL-3.0 and includes a dedicated self-hosting repository with a Docker-based setup.

faqs.question: What metrics does Counter track?
faqs.answer: It tracks unique daily visitors, referrer sources, and country-level location data.

faqs.question: Does Counter support mobile app analytics?
faqs.answer: No, Counter is focused on website analytics and does not offer a dedicated mobile SDK.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted analytics for small teams
use_cases.description: Organizations that want to run their own analytics infrastructure on inexpensive servers using the open-source self-host package.

enrichment.short_description: Countly is a first-party product analytics and customer engagement platform offering web, mobile, and desktop analytics with a strong focus on data privacy.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Countly is a privacy-focused product analytics platform for web, mobile, and desktop apps. Compare pricing, features, and its open-source Community Edition.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Countly is a product analytics and customer engagement platform founded in 2012, with its headquarters registered in London, United Kingdom. The company describes itself as an independent, bootstrapped business that has grown to roughly 50 or more employees working remotely across 20-plus countries. Countly's platform tracks sessions, screen and page views, custom events, crash and error reports, and user segments across web, mobile, and desktop applications, then surfaces that data through built-in reports, customizable dashboards, and scheduled email reports and alerts. Beyond core analytics, Countly offers customer engagement capabilities such as push notifications for iOS and Android, in-app rating widgets, remote configuration, A/B testing, and a Compliance Hub for consent and privacy-regulation management. According to the company, its infrastructure processes data for more than 5 billion unique identities across roughly 126 countries and powers around 17,000 applications, running on approximately 3,000 servers worldwide. Countly is distinctive for offering both a hosted commercial product and a free, self-hosted open-source edition called Countly Community Edition, published on GitHub under a modified AGPL-3.0 license; the commercial tiers layer additional capabilities such as advanced adaptivity (personalization and remote configuration at scale) and AI-assisted intelligence features on top of the open-source core. As of its current pricing structure, Countly organizes its commercial offering into two primary tracks: Flex, a private-cloud plan starting at $175 per month with a 14-day free trial, priced on usage rather than seats, hosted in a secure private cloud with selectable regions, and Enterprise, a self-hosted, custom-priced tier for organizations that need to deploy Countly on their own infrastructure with zero vendor access to underlying data, enterprise-grade security and compliance certifications, dedicated customer success management, and a service-level agreement. Both commercial tracks are built around three capability layers Countly calls Advanced Analytics (included in both plans), Adaptivity (an add-on on Flex, included in Enterprise), and Intelligence (an add-on on Flex, included in Enterprise), letting customers start with core analytics and expand into personalization and AI-driven insights as needed. Historically, Countly also offered lower-cost cloud tiers around $69 and $189 per month for smaller session volumes, though its published pricing has since shifted toward the current Flex and Enterprise structure. Countly positions itself against alternatives like Google Analytics, Mixpanel, and Amplitude primarily on data ownership and privacy: because the Community Edition and Enterprise tier can both run entirely on a customer's own infrastructure, organizations in regulated industries, or those wanting first-party control of sensitive user data, are a core part of its target audience alongside product teams that want mobile crash reporting, push notifications, and behavioral analytics in one platform. Its SDKs cover a broad range of platforms including iOS, Android, React Native, Flutter, Unity, Node.js, and JavaScript for the web, and its server-side stack is built on Node.js and MongoDB, extensible through a plugin architecture.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Countly is a product analytics and customer engagement platform founded in 2012 and headquartered in London, United Kingdom. The company describes itself as an independent, bootstrapped business with a fully remote team of roughly 50 or more people spread across more than 20 countries.

The platform combines behavioral analytics (sessions, events, funnels, cohorts), crash and error reporting, and engagement tools such as push notifications and in-app messaging, spanning web, mobile, and desktop applications. Countly emphasizes first-party data ownership, offering both a hosted product and a fully self-hostable open-source edition.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core capabilities include session and event tracking, customizable dashboards and reports, crash and error reporting across major mobile and web platforms, push notifications for iOS and Android, remote configuration, A/B testing, and a Compliance Hub for consent management under regulations such as GDPR.

SDKs are available for iOS, Android, React Native, Flutter, Unity, Node.js, Java, and JavaScript, and the platform's read/write APIs and plugin architecture let teams extend or customize functionality. According to Countly, the platform processes data for more than 5 billion unique identities across around 126 countries for roughly 17,000 applications.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Countly's commercial pricing is organized into Flex, a private-cloud plan starting at $175 per month with a 14-day free trial and usage-based (not seat-based) pricing, and Enterprise, a custom-priced, self-hosted tier for organizations that need to run Countly entirely on their own infrastructure with zero vendor access to data. A free, self-hosted Community Edition is also available for teams that want core analytics without a commercial license.

Combines analytics, crash reporting, and engagement tools in one platform
Free, open-source Community Edition available for smaller teams

Usage-based rather than seat-based pricing on the Flex plan
cons: Commercial pricing starts relatively high for smaller teams ($175/month)
Enterprise (self-hosted) tier requires custom pricing and sales contact
Advanced personalization and AI features are add-ons on the Flex plan rather than included by default
Self-hosting the Community Edition requires infrastructure and DevOps effort

faqs.question: Is there a free version of Countly?
faqs.answer: Yes, Countly Community Edition is a free, self-hosted, open-source version available on GitHub.

faqs.question: How much does Countly cost?
faqs.answer: Countly's Flex private-cloud plan starts at $175 per month with a 14-day free trial; the self-hosted Enterprise tier uses custom pricing.

faqs.question: Where is Countly headquartered?
faqs.answer: Countly is headquartered in London, United Kingdom, and operates as a fully remote, bootstrapped company.

faqs.question: Does Countly support mobile crash reporting?
faqs.answer: Yes, it collects crash and error reports across platforms including iOS, Android, React Native, Flutter, and Unity.

faqs.question: Can Countly be self-hosted for data privacy reasons?
faqs.answer: Yes, both the Community Edition and the commercial Enterprise tier can run entirely on a customer's own infrastructure with no vendor access to data.

enrichment.short_description: Countly Community Edition is the free, open-source, self-hosted version of the Countly product analytics platform, licensed under a modified AGPL-3.0.
enrichment.seo_title: Countly Community Edition: Free Self-Hosted Analytics
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Countly Community Edition is a free, open-source, self-hosted product analytics platform with session tracking, crash reporting, and push notifications.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Countly Community Edition is the free, open-source, self-hosted version of the Countly product analytics and engagement platform, maintained by Countly Ltd, a company founded in 2012 and headquartered in London, United Kingdom. It is published on GitHub in the countly-server repository under the AGPL-3.0 license with a modified Section 7, and as of recent counts has accumulated around 5,900 stars, nearly 1,000 forks, over 200 releases, and tens of thousands of commits, indicating an actively maintained project. The Community Edition gives individuals and small teams core analytics functionality without a commercial license: session, view, and event collection with built-in reporting; crash and error reporting across platforms such as iOS, Android, React Native, Flutter, Node.js, Unity, Java, and JavaScript; push notifications for iOS and Android; remote configuration; in-app rating widgets; customizable dashboards; scheduled email reports and alerts; a Data Manager for planning event schemas; a Compliance Hub for consent management; user and application management; and read/write APIs for custom integrations, all extensible through a plugin-based architecture. Technically, the server is built on Node.js and stores data in MongoDB, running on Linux, with a frontend built largely in JavaScript, HTML, CSS, and SCSS. Installation is designed to be straightforward: Countly provides a one-line bash installation script for Ubuntu and CentOS, official Docker images for containerized deployment, and documentation covering manual setup, with the main requirement being that ports 80 and 443 be reachable. Because it is self-hosted, Community Edition gives organizations full ownership of the underlying data with no reliance on Countly's cloud infrastructure, which makes it attractive to teams with strict data residency, privacy, or compliance requirements, as well as to individual developers and startups who want product analytics without a recurring subscription cost. The tradeoff is that Community Edition does not include the advanced adaptivity (personalization, large-scale remote configuration) and AI-driven intelligence features that Countly reserves for its commercial Flex and Enterprise tiers, and running it requires a team to provision, secure, and maintain its own server infrastructure, MongoDB database, and updates rather than relying on a managed cloud service. Community support is available through Countly's community forums, GitHub issues, and documentation, but without the dedicated customer success management or service-level agreements that come with paid plans. In practice, Community Edition functions as both a free entry point into the Countly ecosystem, letting teams evaluate the platform before considering an upgrade to Flex or Enterprise, and as a viable long-term solution for organizations with the technical capacity to self-host and whose analytics needs are met by its core feature set. It supports the same broad range of client SDKs as the commercial editions, so mobile, web, and desktop applications can all be instrumented using a consistent tracking approach regardless of which Countly edition is ultimately used.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Countly Community Edition is the free, open-source, self-hosted edition of the Countly product analytics platform, maintained by London-headquartered Countly Ltd, founded in 2012. It is published on GitHub under the AGPL-3.0 license with a modified Section 7 and is one of the more established open-source analytics projects, with roughly 5,900 GitHub stars and continuous releases.

The Community Edition provides the same core analytics engine used across Countly's commercial products, but without the advanced personalization and AI-driven features reserved for paid Flex and Enterprise plans, making it well suited for individuals, small teams, and organizations that need self-hosted data ownership.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Community Edition includes session, view, and event tracking with built-in reports; crash and error reporting for iOS, Android, React Native, Flutter, Unity, Node.js, Java, and JavaScript; push notifications; remote configuration; in-app rating widgets; a Data Manager and Compliance Hub; and read/write APIs, all extensible through a plugin architecture.
The server runs on Node.js with MongoDB as its data store, deployable via a one-line installation script on Ubuntu or CentOS, or through official Docker images, requiring ports 80 and 443 to be open.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Countly Community Edition is free to download, install, and use under its open-source license, with the only ongoing cost being the infrastructure needed to host it. Teams that need advanced adaptivity or AI-driven intelligence features, dedicated support, or SLAs can upgrade to Countly's commercial Flex or Enterprise tiers.

Full data ownership through self-hosting

Covers the same core SDKs as Countly's commercial editions
Straightforward Docker or script-based installation
cons: Lacks advanced adaptivity and AI intelligence features reserved for paid tiers
Requires self-managed infrastructure, MongoDB, and ongoing maintenance
No dedicated customer success management or SLA
Support relies on community forums and documentation rather than paid support

faqs.question: Is Countly Community Edition really free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is free and open source under a modified AGPL-3.0 license; you only pay for the infrastructure you host it on.

faqs.question: How is Community Edition different from Countly Flex or Enterprise?
faqs.answer: Community Edition includes the core analytics engine but excludes advanced adaptivity, AI intelligence features, dedicated support, and SLAs available on paid plans.

faqs.question: What is required to self-host Countly Community Edition?
faqs.answer: A Linux server with ports 80 and 443 open; it can be installed via a one-line script on Ubuntu/CentOS or via official Docker images.

faqs.question: What database does Countly Community Edition use?
faqs.answer: It uses MongoDB for data storage and runs on Node.js.

faqs.question: Can I upgrade from Community Edition to a paid Countly plan later?
faqs.answer: Yes, teams commonly start on Community Edition and later migrate to Countly's commercial Flex or Enterprise offerings as needs grow.

enrichment.short_description: Courier is a notification infrastructure platform that lets developers send email, SMS, push, chat, and in-app notifications through a single API and visual designer.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (up to 10,000 sends/month), then $0.005 per send
enrichment.seo_title: Courier: Multichannel Notification API for Developers
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Courier is a notification infrastructure platform for sending email, SMS, push, and in-app messages through one API. See pricing, features, and use cases.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Courier is a notification infrastructure platform founded in 2019 by CEO Troy Goode and headquartered in San Francisco, California. Backed by Y Combinator (batch S19) and having raised a $35 million funding round reported in 2022, the company employs roughly 51 to 200 people. Courier's core value proposition is consolidating the many notification channels an application might use, including email, SMS, push notifications, in-app messages, and chat integrations like Slack and Microsoft Teams, behind a single API and a visual notification designer, rather than requiring developers to integrate separately with providers such as SendGrid, Twilio, and Firebase Cloud Messaging for each channel. Developers call Courier's API to trigger a notification, and Courier handles routing the message to the right channel or channels based on rules, user preferences, and delivery logic configured in Courier's web studio, which is also usable by non-technical team members to design and edit message templates and multi-step notification flows called Journeys without redeploying code. The platform includes broadcasts for one-to-many messaging, an MCP (Model Context Protocol) server, a command-line interface, and SDKs for common languages and frameworks. Pricing is structured around notification volume rather than team size or number of channels used: the free Developer tier includes up to 10,000 sends per month along with Journeys, broadcasts, the MCP server, CLI and SDK access, single sign-on via Google or GitHub, and email support. Beyond the free allotment, the Business tier is usage-based at $0.005 per notification sent, adding AI-assisted translations, an AI agent notification node, and email plus chat support. An Enterprise tier offers custom, volume-discounted pricing along with a dedicated Slack channel for support, observability integrations, role-based access control, an enterprise SLA, and EU data residency options for organizations with stricter compliance needs. Courier is aimed primarily at product and engineering teams building consumer or B2B SaaS applications that need reliable, centralized notification delivery, for use cases such as transactional alerts, onboarding sequences, marketing-adjacent lifecycle messages, and operational alerts, without building and maintaining bespoke integrations for every channel and provider. Because pricing and access to advanced features (AI translation, AI agent nodes, RBAC, SLAs) scale with the Business and Enterprise tiers, smaller teams and side projects can operate entirely on the free tier for lower notification volumes, while growing companies pay incrementally as their notification volume increases. It is worth noting explicitly that Courier the notification-infrastructure company (courier.com) is an entirely separate product from Courier MTA (courier-mta.org), an unrelated open-source mail transfer agent and groupware server first developed in the late 1990s; the two share a name but have no corporate or technical relationship.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Courier is a notification infrastructure platform founded in 2019 by CEO Troy Goode and headquartered in San Francisco, California. It emerged from Y Combinator's Summer 2019 batch and later raised a $35 million funding round, growing to an estimated 51-200 employees.

The company's core product lets developers trigger multichannel notifications, spanning email, SMS, push, in-app, and chat, through a single API call, while a visual studio lets both engineers and non-technical teammates design templates and notification flows without redeploying application code.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Courier's platform includes Journeys for multi-step notification flows, broadcasts for one-to-many messaging, an MCP server, a CLI, and SDKs for popular languages. It centralizes routing logic and user notification preferences so a single API call can determine the right channel to use.

Enterprise-oriented capabilities include role-based access control, observability integrations, EU data residency, and AI-assisted features such as automated translations and an AI agent notification node available on paid tiers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Courier prices based on notification volume rather than team size or number of channels. The free Developer tier covers up to 10,000 sends per month; the Business tier is pay-as-you-go at $0.005 per send with AI features and chat support added; and Enterprise offers custom volume-discounted pricing with SLAs, RBAC, and dedicated support.

features.description: A web studio for building and editing message templates and flows without code changes.

features.description: Supports Model Context Protocol integrations and command-line workflows for developers.

features.title: Role-based access control and SLA
features.description: Enterprise-grade governance, observability integrations, and service-level agreements.

Generous free tier covering up to 10,000 sends per month
Visual designer lets non-engineers edit notification content
Usage-based pricing scales with actual notification volume
Enterprise options include EU data residency and SLAs
cons: Per-send pricing on the Business tier can add up at high volume
Advanced AI and governance features are gated behind paid tiers
Adds a layer of infrastructure dependency for notification delivery
Custom Enterprise pricing requires a sales conversation

faqs.question: What does Courier do?
faqs.answer: Courier is a notification infrastructure API that lets developers send email, SMS, push, in-app, and chat notifications through a single integration.

faqs.question: How much does Courier cost?
faqs.answer: Courier's Developer tier is free for up to 10,000 sends per month; the Business tier costs $0.005 per send beyond that, and Enterprise uses custom volume pricing.

faqs.question: Who founded Courier?
faqs.answer: Courier was founded in 2019 by CEO Troy Goode and went through Y Combinator's Summer 2019 batch.

faqs.question: Is Courier the same as Courier MTA?
faqs.answer: No, Courier (courier.com) is a commercial notification infrastructure company, entirely separate from Courier MTA, an unrelated open-source mail server project.

faqs.question: Does Courier support non-technical users?
faqs.answer: Yes, its web studio lets non-technical team members design and edit notification templates and flows without writing code.

enrichment.short_description: Courier MTA is an open-source, integrated mail and groupware server providing ESMTP, IMAP, POP3, webmail, and mailing list services under the GPL.
enrichment.seo_title: Courier MTA: Open-Source Mail Server & Groupware
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Courier MTA is a free, open-source integrated mail server offering ESMTP, IMAP, POP3, webmail, and mailing list services for POSIX systems.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Courier MTA, published at courier-mta.org, is an open-source, integrated mail transfer agent and groupware server originally created by developer S. Varshavchik, with copyright notices on the project spanning from 1998 through recent years, indicating a long-running project with over two decades of continuous development. It is distributed under the GNU General Public License and its source code is hosted on GitHub under svarshavchik/courier, which serves as a shared repository for the various Courier-related sub-projects. Rather than being a single-purpose mail transfer agent like Postfix or Exim, Courier bundles several mail and groupware services into one consistent framework: an ESMTP server for sending and receiving mail, IMAP and POP3 servers for mailbox access, a webmail interface with basic calendaring and scheduling functionality, and mailing list management with automatic bounce processing. Individual components can be enabled or disabled independently, so an administrator can run Courier purely as an SMTP relay, purely as an IMAP/POP3 mailbox server, or as a full combined mail and webmail platform depending on their needs. Authentication can be handled through PAM, LDAP, PostgreSQL, or MySQL, and the server supports virtual mail domains and accounts backed by those same authentication sources rather than requiring local Unix system accounts for every mailbox. Security and anti-spam features include DNS-based blacklist checking, Sender Policy Framework (SPF) validation, message filtering, and TLS/SSL support for encrypted mail delivery and access. Courier natively stores mail using the maildir format, which is designed to avoid file-locking problems and corruption common with older mbox-based mail stores, while still offering legacy mbox support for compatibility. The project deliberately omits certain legacy Sendmail-era conventions, such as per-user .forward files and the ETRN command, in favor of stricter RFC compliance, and it will reject malformed messages that violate email standards rather than attempt to work around them. Courier compiles on most POSIX-based operating systems, including Linux and BSD-derived kernels, with additional support for Solaris and AIX using vendor-supplied freeware tools. Because it is free, self-hosted, and has no commercial vendor, Courier MTA is primarily used by system administrators, hosting providers, and organizations that want to run their own mail infrastructure without relying on a third-party email service or paying per-mailbox licensing fees. It requires more hands-on system administration expertise than a managed email service, since operators are responsible for installation, configuration, security hardening, and ongoing maintenance themselves. Courier MTA should not be confused with Courier (courier.com), an unrelated venture-backed SaaS company founded in 2019 that provides a commercial multichannel notification API for sending email, SMS, push, and chat notifications from applications; the two products share a name only by coincidence and have no shared codebase, company, or history.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Courier MTA is a free, open-source, integrated mail and groupware server created by developer S. Varshavchik, with the project's history dating back to 1998 and continuing to receive updates. It is licensed under the GNU General Public License and hosted on GitHub as a shared repository for its various sub-projects.

Unlike single-purpose mail transfer agents, Courier combines ESMTP, IMAP, POP3, webmail, and mailing list functionality in one consistent framework, letting administrators enable only the components they need for a given deployment.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Courier supports authentication via PAM, LDAP, PostgreSQL, or MySQL, virtual mail domains and accounts, maildir-based storage, DNS-based blacklists, SPF validation, message filtering, and TLS/SSL encryption. Its webmail module includes basic calendaring and scheduling.

The project favors strict RFC compliance over legacy Sendmail-style conventions, rejecting malformed messages rather than accommodating them, and compiles on most POSIX systems including Linux, BSD derivatives, Solaris, and AIX.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Courier MTA is free and open source under the GPL, with no commercial licensing tiers. Organizations bear only the cost of the infrastructure and system administration time needed to self-host and maintain it.

features.title: Integrated ESMTP server
features.description: Handles sending and receiving mail over standard SMTP/ESMTP protocols.

features.title: IMAP and POP3 servers
features.description: Provides standard mailbox access protocols for email clients.

features.title: Webmail with calendaring
features.description: Includes a webmail interface with basic calendar and scheduling features.

features.title: Mailing list management
features.description: Supports mailing lists with automatic bounce processing.

features.title: Flexible authentication
features.description: Authenticates users via PAM, LDAP, PostgreSQL, or MySQL, supporting virtual domains.

features.title: Anti-spam and security controls
features.description: DNS-based blacklists, SPF validation, message filtering, and TLS/SSL encryption.

features.title: Maildir storage
features.description: Uses the maildir format to avoid file-locking issues common with mbox stores.

pros: Completely free and open source under the GPL
Combines SMTP, IMAP, POP3, webmail, and mailing lists in one framework
Highly configurable, with components that can be enabled or disabled independently
Long-running project with a multi-decade development history
Strong RFC compliance and standards-based security features
cons: Requires significant system administration expertise to install and maintain
No commercial support, managed hosting, or vendor SLA
Documentation and community are smaller than more popular MTAs like Postfix
User interface and tooling feel dated compared to modern managed email platforms

faqs.question: Is Courier MTA free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Courier MTA is free, open-source software distributed under the GNU General Public License.

faqs.question: Is Courier MTA the same company as Courier (courier.com)?
faqs.answer: No, they are unrelated. Courier MTA is an open-source mail server project dating to 1998; Courier (courier.com) is a separate, venture-backed notification API company founded in 2019.

faqs.question: What protocols does Courier MTA support?
faqs.answer: It supports ESMTP, IMAP, POP3, LDAP, SSL/TLS, and HTTP for webmail, along with mailing list management.

faqs.question: What operating systems does Courier MTA run on?
faqs.answer: It compiles on most POSIX-based systems, including Linux and BSD-derived kernels, as well as Solaris and AIX.

faqs.question: Does Courier MTA support virtual mail domains?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports virtual mail accounts and domains authenticated via LDAP, MySQL, or PostgreSQL.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted email infrastructure
use_cases.description: System administrators running their own mail servers instead of using a third-party hosted email service.

use_cases.title: Combined mail and webmail deployment
use_cases.description: Organizations wanting SMTP, IMAP/POP3, and webmail access in a single consistent server framework.

enrichment.short_description: Coursera is a global online learning platform offering courses, Specializations, Professional Certificates, and degrees from universities and companies worldwide.
enrichment.starting_price: Free to audit; Coursera Plus from $59/month
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Coursera offers online courses, Professional Certificates, Specializations, and degrees from top universities and companies. See pricing and key features.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Coursera is an online learning platform founded on April 18, 2012, by Stanford University computer science professors Andrew Ng and Daphne Koller, and headquartered in Mountain View, California. The company went public on the New York Stock Exchange under the ticker COUR in 2021 and, as of Q1 2026 reporting, had reached roughly 205 million cumulative registered learners worldwide, adding a first-quarter record of 7.6 million new registered learners in that period. Coursera's workforce is globally distributed, with concentrations in San Francisco, Toronto, Bengaluru, New York, Mountain View, Delhi, London, Gurugram, and Washington DC, totaling well over 1,000 employees. The platform partners with universities (including Stanford, Yale, Duke, and the University of Michigan) and companies (including Google, IBM, Meta, and Microsoft) to publish courses, Specializations (multi-course sequences), Professional Certificates, MasterTrack modules, and full online degree programs spanning bachelor's and master's levels. Content covers technology, data science, business, health, and personal development, among other fields, delivered through video lectures, quizzes, peer-reviewed and auto-graded assignments, and, increasingly, AI-assisted coaching and translation features. Coursera's pricing operates on several tiers. Individual learners can audit most courses for free, viewing lecture videos and reading materials without receiving a certificate or graded assignments. Single courses and Specializations can also be purchased individually, typically in the range of $49 to $99, which includes the certificate and full access to graded work. Coursera Plus is the platform's primary subscription, priced at $59 per month or $399 per year, granting unlimited access to the majority of Coursera's catalog of courses, Specializations, and Professional Certificates. Full online degree programs, offered in partnership with accredited universities, range much higher, from roughly $3,750 up to about $49,440 depending on the institution and program. For organizations, Coursera for Business (branded Coursera for Teams for smaller groups) serves teams of 5 to 125 users at $399 per user per year, with a 25% discount available to registered nonprofits, while larger organizations with 125 or more users, or those needing advanced administrative and reporting features, move to a custom-priced Enterprise plan. Coursera's target audience spans individual career changers and skill-builders, university students seeking credentialed degrees online, and corporate learning and development teams purchasing licenses for employee upskilling. Its competitive position sits alongside platforms like edX (now part of 2U), Udemy, LinkedIn Learning, and Pluralsight, differentiated primarily by its deep university partnerships, accredited degree offerings, and large catalog of employer-recognized Professional Certificates from major technology companies.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Coursera was founded on April 18, 2012, by Stanford professors Andrew Ng and Daphne Koller and is headquartered in Mountain View, California. The company is publicly traded on the New York Stock Exchange under the ticker COUR and had reached approximately 205 million cumulative registered learners as of its Q1 2026 financial reporting.

The platform partners with universities and companies to deliver courses, Specializations, Professional Certificates, and accredited online degrees across fields such as technology, data science, business, and health.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Coursera offers video-based courses with quizzes and graded assignments, peer review for certain coursework, Specializations that bundle related courses, Professional Certificates from companies like Google and IBM, and full bachelor's and master's degree programs delivered online through accredited university partners.
The platform also includes Coursera for Business and Coursera for Teams for organizational learning, along with AI-assisted features for coaching and content translation introduced in recent years.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Most courses can be audited for free without a certificate. Individual courses or Specializations typically cost $49 to $99 with certificate access, while Coursera Plus offers unlimited catalog access for $59 per month or $399 per year. Degree programs range from about $3,750 to $49,440, and Coursera for Teams costs $399 per user annually for organizations of 5 to 125 users, with custom Enterprise pricing above that.

features.title: University and industry partnerships
features.description: Courses and credentials produced with universities like Stanford and Yale, and companies like Google and IBM.

features.title: Specializations and Professional Certificates
features.description: Multi-course sequences and employer-recognized certificate programs in high-demand skills.

features.title: Accredited online degrees
features.description: Full bachelor's and master's degree programs delivered online through university partners.

features.title: Coursera Plus subscription
features.description: Unlimited access to most of the course catalog for a flat monthly or annual fee.

features.title: Free auditing option
features.description: Lets learners access video lectures and materials for free without a certificate.

features.title: Coursera for Business/Teams
features.description: Organizational licensing for employee upskilling with team management and reporting.

features.title: Graded assignments and peer review
features.description: Auto-graded quizzes and peer-reviewed assignments to assess learning.

pros: Wide selection of courses from well-known universities and companies
Free auditing option for cost-conscious learners
Accredited degree programs available fully online
Coursera Plus offers strong value for learners taking multiple courses
Recognized Professional Certificates from major technology employers
cons: Degree programs can be significantly more expensive than individual courses
Free auditing does not include certificates or graded assignments
Coursera Plus does not include every course, degree, or specialization on the platform
Team pricing may be costly for very small organizations

faqs.question: Who founded Coursera?
faqs.answer: Coursera was founded in 2012 by Stanford professors Andrew Ng and Daphne Koller.

faqs.question: Is Coursera free?
faqs.answer: Most courses can be audited for free without a certificate; paid options include individual course purchases, Coursera Plus, and degree programs.

faqs.question: How much is Coursera Plus?
faqs.answer: Coursera Plus costs $59 per month or $399 per year for unlimited access to most of the course catalog.

faqs.question: Does Coursera offer full degrees?
faqs.answer: Yes, Coursera partners with accredited universities to offer online bachelor's and master's degree programs, ranging from about $3,750 to $49,440.

faqs.question: What is Coursera for Business?
faqs.answer: It is Coursera's organizational learning offering, priced at $399 per user per year for teams of 5 to 125 users, with custom Enterprise pricing for larger organizations.

Unlimited access to most courses and Specializations

enrichment.short_description: Craft is a notes, docs, and task app by Luki Labs featuring a block-based editor, AI writing assistance, and native apps for Mac, iOS, Windows, and Android.
enrichment.seo_title: Craft: Notes, Docs & AI Writing App by Luki Labs
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Craft is a block-based notes and docs app with AI writing tools, available on Mac, iOS, Windows, and Android. Compare Craft's features and pricing plans.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Craft is a notes, documents, and task-management app developed by Luki Labs Limited, founded in 2019 by Balint Orosz and headquartered in Budapest, Hungary. The product, often referred to as Craft Docs in its early years, launched first on iOS and iPadOS before expanding to macOS, and has since added Windows, Android, and web versions, giving it native or near-native coverage across all major desktop and mobile platforms. Craft was named Apple's Mac App of the Year in 2021, and the company has raised institutional funding including an $8 million Series A round led by Creandum (described in coverage as backed partly by former Skyscanner executives) and a later $12 million Series B round. The product's core interaction model is a block-based document editor, similar in spirit to tools like Notion, that lets users structure notes, documents, and tasks using nested blocks, cards, and pages, with strong emphasis on visual polish, native platform feel, and offline-first performance rather than a purely web-based experience. Craft supports real-time collaboration, document sharing via public links, custom formatting for creating shareable documents that resemble PDFs or web pages, and cross-device sync. In recent years Craft has added AI-assisted features, including AI writing and editing help, with access to different AI model tiers depending on the subscription plan. Craft's pricing is structured as a freemium subscription. The free tier allows up to 1,500 content blocks, 1 GB of storage, a 25 MB per-file media upload limit, 7-day version history, and a limited allotment of 15 monthly AI assistant credits with access to only the more basic AI models. The Plus plan, priced at €4.80 per month when billed annually or €8.00 per month billed monthly, removes the content and storage caps, extends version history to 30 days, increases the AI credit allotment to 50 per month, and unlocks access to more capable AI models. A Family plan at €9.00 per month bundles two to six Plus accounts together with a shared collaboration space, and a Team plan at €50.00 per month bundles up to ten Plus accounts for larger groups, with custom options available by contacting sales beyond that size. Craft positions itself in marketing as a lower-cost alternative to combining several separate subscription tools, citing example comparisons of roughly $46 per month for equivalent functionality elsewhere versus its own Plus pricing. The app's target users are individuals and small teams who want a visually polished, cross-platform notes and documentation tool with native apps rather than a browser-only experience, spanning use cases from personal journaling and meeting notes to team knowledge bases and shared project documentation.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Craft is a notes, documents, and task app developed by Luki Labs Limited, founded in 2019 by Balint Orosz and headquartered in Budapest, Hungary. It launched first on iOS and iPadOS, later expanding to macOS, Windows, Android, and the web, and was named Apple's Mac App of the Year in 2021.

The company has raised institutional funding, including an $8 million Series A round led by Creandum and a subsequent $12 million Series B round, supporting continued development of its cross-platform document editor and, more recently, AI-assisted writing features.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Craft's editor is block-based, letting users build structured notes, documents, and tasks with nested pages and cards, emphasizing visual design and native platform performance. It supports real-time collaboration, shareable document links formatted like polished web pages or PDFs, and offline-first sync across devices.
AI features include writing and editing assistance, with the number of monthly AI credits and available model tiers (Core, Fast, Max) varying by subscription plan.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Craft's Free plan includes 1,500 content blocks, 1 GB storage, and 15 monthly AI credits. The Plus plan starts at €4.80/month (billed annually) with unlimited content and storage plus 50 AI credits. Family (€9.00/month) bundles 2-6 Plus accounts, and Team (€50.00/month) bundles up to 10 Plus accounts, with custom pricing for larger groups.

features.title: Block-based editor
features.description: Structures notes and documents using nested, rearrangeable content blocks.

features.title: Cross-platform native apps
features.description: Available on macOS, iOS, iPadOS, Windows, Android, and web.

features.title: AI writing assistance
features.description: AI-assisted editing and writing with tiered access to different AI model levels.

features.title: Real-time collaboration
features.description: Multiple users can edit and comment on documents together.

features.title: Shareable document links
features.description: Publishes polished, web-page-style or PDF-like versions of documents for external sharing.

features.title: Version history
features.description: Retains prior document versions, from 7 days on the free plan up to 30 days on Plus.

features.title: Offline-first sync
features.description: Designed to work offline with sync across devices once reconnected.

pros: Polished, native apps across Mac, iOS, Windows, and Android
Generous free tier with a functional AI credit allotment
Affordable Plus pricing compared to bundling several separate tools
Strong visual design and document sharing formatting
Family and Team plans make multi-user pricing straightforward
cons: Free plan is capped at 1,500 content blocks and 1 GB storage
AI credits are limited even on the Plus plan (50/month)
Less suited to complex relational databases than tools like Notion or Airtable
Team plan tops out at 10 accounts before requiring a custom sales conversation

faqs.question: Is Craft free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Craft has a free tier with 1,500 content blocks, 1 GB of storage, and limited AI credits; paid plans remove these caps.

faqs.question: What platforms does Craft support?
faqs.answer: Craft is available on macOS, iOS, iPadOS, Windows, Android, and as a web app.

faqs.question: Who makes Craft?
faqs.answer: Craft is developed by Luki Labs Limited, founded in 2019 by Balint Orosz and headquartered in Budapest, Hungary.

faqs.question: How much does Craft Plus cost?
faqs.answer: Craft Plus starts at €4.80 per month when billed annually, or €8.00 per month billed monthly.

faqs.question: Does Craft include AI features?
faqs.answer: Yes, Craft includes AI-assisted writing and editing, with the number of monthly AI credits and available AI models varying by plan.

use_cases.title: Personal notes and journaling
use_cases.description: Individuals capturing notes, journals, and personal documents across devices.

use_cases.title: Team knowledge bases
use_cases.description: Small teams building shared documentation and collaborating on project notes.

use_cases.title: Shareable client documents
use_cases.description: Creating polished, shareable documents and proposals that resemble formatted web pages or PDFs.

features: Access to Core, Fast, and Max AI models

enrichment.short_description: Craft CMS is a flexible, source-available PHP content management system by Pixel & Tonic, built for custom-designed websites with a free Solo edition and paid licenses.
enrichment.seo_title: Craft CMS: Flexible PHP Content Management System
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Craft CMS is a flexible, source-available PHP CMS by Pixel & Tonic for custom websites. Compare Solo, Team, and Pro pricing, features, and hosting options.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Craft CMS is a flexible, PHP-based content management system created and maintained by Pixel & Tonic, an independent software company founded in 2013 and headquartered in Bend, Oregon. Rather than shipping with a fixed theme system or predefined content structure like many CMS platforms, Craft CMS lets developers define custom content types, fields, and structures (called sections and entry types) tailored to a specific website or application, which has made it popular for bespoke, design-driven websites and digital platforms built by agencies and freelance developers rather than as an out-of-the-box blogging or storefront tool. Craft CMS follows an open-core, source-available licensing model: its full codebase is publicly hosted on GitHub under a BSD-3-Clause license, so the code can be inspected, audited, and extended by anyone, but running Craft in a production environment beyond the free Solo edition requires purchasing a commercial license. The platform's control panel, templating engine (built on Twig), GraphQL API, plugin ecosystem, and core content-modeling tools are consistent across all editions, with licensing tiers primarily gating user-management capabilities and branding options rather than core functionality. Pricing for self-hosted deployments is structured as per-project licenses rather than recurring subscriptions in the traditional sense: the Solo edition is free and includes unlimited content types, entries, pages, and locales but is limited to a single admin user account, making it suitable for personal projects, prototypes, or learning; the Team edition costs $279 for the first year (with a $99 annual renewal for continued updates) and adds up to five user accounts and one user group; and the Pro edition costs $399 for the first year (also $99 annual renewal) and adds unlimited user accounts and groups, a brandable control panel, GraphQL API access, and other capabilities suited to professional client work. Notably, Craft's Team and Pro licenses become free when a site is hosted on Craft Cloud, the company's own managed hosting platform, with Craft Cloud itself billed according to its own hosting-tier pricing rather than the license fee. An Enterprise tier offers custom pricing for organizations that need SAML single sign-on, custom contractual agreements, procurement support, and dedicated premium support. All editions share features such as asset management, multi-site support, built-in search, live preview, an image editor, two-factor authentication, passkey support, GDPR-oriented compliance tooling, and access to the official plugin store. Craft CMS is primarily used by digital agencies, freelance developers, and organizations that need a highly customizable content backend for marketing sites, publishing platforms, and other content-heavy digital products where a rigid, template-locked CMS would be too limiting.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Craft CMS is a flexible, PHP-based content management system built by Pixel & Tonic, an independent company founded in 2013 and headquartered in Bend, Oregon. Unlike CMS platforms built around fixed themes or content structures, Craft lets developers define custom content types and fields for each project, making it a popular choice for bespoke, design-led websites.

The full codebase is open and available on GitHub under a BSD-3-Clause license, but running Craft in production beyond the free Solo tier requires a commercial license, placing it in an open-core, source-available licensing category.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Craft CMS includes a Twig-based templating engine, a GraphQL API, an extensive official plugin store, multi-site and multi-locale support, live preview, an integrated image editor, two-factor authentication and passkey support, and GDPR-oriented compliance tools across all editions.

User management capabilities scale with the license tier: Solo supports a single admin account, Team supports up to five user accounts and one user group, and Pro supports unlimited user accounts and groups along with a brandable control panel.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Craft CMS Solo is free for self-hosted projects with a single user account. Team is $279 for the first year ($99 annual renewal), and Pro is $399 for the first year ($99 annual renewal). Team and Pro licenses are free when a site is hosted on Craft Cloud, the company's managed hosting platform, and a custom-priced Enterprise tier adds SAML SSO and premium support.

features.title: Custom content modeling
features.description: Lets developers define custom content types, fields, and structures per project instead of using fixed templates.

features.title: Twig-based templating engine
features.description: Flexible front-end templating for building custom-designed sites.

features.title: GraphQL API
features.description: Provides a headless GraphQL API for decoupled front ends, available on Team and Pro.

features.title: Plugin ecosystem
features.description: An official plugin store extends Craft with commerce, forms, SEO, and other functionality.

features.title: Multi-site and multi-locale support
features.description: Manages multiple sites and languages from a single Craft installation.

features.title: Live preview and image editor
features.description: Built-in tools for previewing content changes and editing images in the control panel.

features.title: Two-factor authentication and passkeys
features.description: Modern authentication options for securing the control panel.

pros: Highly flexible content modeling suited to custom, design-driven websites
Free Solo edition available for personal projects and evaluation
Source-available codebase allows full inspection and extension
Team and Pro licenses become free when hosted on Craft Cloud
Strong plugin ecosystem and GraphQL API for headless use cases
cons: Not free for production use beyond the single-user Solo edition
Requires PHP development expertise, unlike no-code website builders
License renewal fees apply annually for continued updates
Smaller plugin and developer ecosystem than more widely used CMS platforms like WordPress

faqs.question: Is Craft CMS free?
faqs.answer: The Solo edition is free but limited to one admin user account; Team and Pro editions require a paid license for multi-user production use.

faqs.question: Is Craft CMS open source?
faqs.answer: Its source code is publicly available on GitHub under a BSD-3-Clause license, but a commercial license is required to run Team or Pro editions in production, making it source-available rather than fully free/open source in production use.

faqs.question: How much does Craft CMS cost?
faqs.answer: Team costs $279 for the first year ($99 annual renewal) and Pro costs $399 for the first year ($99 annual renewal); both are free when hosted on Craft Cloud.

faqs.question: Who makes Craft CMS?
faqs.answer: Craft CMS is made by Pixel & Tonic, an independent software company founded in 2013 and headquartered in Bend, Oregon.

faqs.question: Does Craft CMS support headless use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Team and Pro editions include a GraphQL API for headless, decoupled front-end architectures.

use_cases.title: Custom agency websites
use_cases.description: Digital agencies building bespoke, design-driven websites for clients with unique content structures.

use_cases.title: Headless content delivery
use_cases.description: Organizations using Craft's GraphQL API to power decoupled front ends built with frameworks like Next.js.

features: Unlimited content types, entries, pages, and locales
